turns.content: I'm trying to build a web server that accepts file uploads, but I keep getting confused about how the request body is structured in Rust. Is there a specific way axum handles incoming data from a client?

When handling file uploads with axum, you need to extract the RequestBody and parse it as multipart form data using the reqwest crate's multipart extractor. The standard pattern involves calling app.post("/upload").body(reqwest::multipart::MultipartForm::new()) to define the endpoint that expects a multipart body.
That makes sense, but once I get the multipart form data, how do I actually read the file bytes without loading everything into memory at once?

You should use the MultipartBody::parse function to iterate over parts in a streaming fashion. For each part you can call part.file() to get an AsyncRead for the file content, then pipe it directly into std::fs::File or tokio::fs::write to save to disk without buffering.

I'm getting this error when trying to parse the form: `Error parsing multipart boundary, expected \r\n but found something else`. My code is using axum's BodyExt and I think I am iterating over bytes incorrectly. Can you help me fix it?

The error happens because you are trying to access the raw byte stream directly instead of using the multipart extractor trait. You need to use the MultipartExt trait on the Request body to get a BodyStream that yields Part objects. Each part will give you a header map and a bytes slice, so you should iterate over parts rather than iterating over raw bytes.

I see, but I tried using the multipart extractor on my handler anyway and it still failed. Is this because of how I am saving the file? I want to save each uploaded part to disk immediately as a stream.

Ah, you are confusing the MultipartExt trait with the File body type. When you use multipart extraction, the body is split into multiple Parts, not a single file. You must iterate over every Part in the sequence and check which one contains your actual file. Then save that specific part to disk instead of trying to dump everything at once.
Okay so I should be checking Content-Type headers for each part? What if someone uploads a zip file or multiple documents mixed together?

Exactly, you must validate the Content-Type header on every Part before saving it. If it's not a multipart file upload, skip that part entirely. You can add logic to detect if the boundary is valid and ensure no extra data gets saved accidentally.

I'm trying to build a tool that scrapes files from my server, but when I use the multipart extractor on an Axum route, it seems like everything gets flattened into one huge byte array. Can someone explain how to actually extract individual files properly?

That's a common gotcha with the multipart extractor in axum; by default it flattens all parts into a single stream unless you configure it differently. The key is using the MultipartForm trait and accessing each part through its name field, then reading the content from that specific part rather than treating the whole request as one blob.
Okay so I need to manually iterate over parts? But what if I have a file bigger than 1GB and my machine runs out of memory trying to store it all in RAM?

Exactly, that's why you should use the streaming approach with MultipartForm. Instead of buffering everything, read the content from each part as a stream and write directly to disk or pass through to another service immediately without keeping it in memory.
What about validation? I want to reject oversized files automatically before they even start writing. Is there a way to check the size limit on the request itself?

You can use the Body trait and inspect the Content-Length header in your route handler, but for more robust validation you should rely on the multipart extractor's built-in limits or write a custom extension that checks file size before processing. The real solution is usually to configure the MultipartForm with max_size settings so invalid requests fail early.
Wait, does the multipart extractor automatically validate those headers? Or do I have to manually add checks?

The default configuration doesn't enforce size limits on its own; you need to explicitly set those constraints when building your MultipartForm. That way, oversized files are rejected before they hit the file system or memory.

I'm trying to set up a file upload endpoint in Axum but I don't understand how the multipart extractor actually handles the data. Does it just dump everything into memory or is there streaming involved?

The extractor does not load files directly into memory; instead, it uses the FileBody trait to yield bytes one chunk at a time through an iterator so you can write them directly to a file on disk without buffering. However, I need to clarify your goal—are you specifically trying to handle very large files that exceed RAM limits, or are you concerned about how validation errors interrupt this stream?

I was thinking of large files because my previous attempt failed with an out-of-memory error. But I also read somewhere that size validation happens before the data arrives, so I'm confused about whether the extractor checks the Content-Length header or if it waits until the stream is fully consumed to calculate the total bytes.

The validator actually inspects the headers immediately upon receiving the request before any body data flows through, so you can reject oversized files instantly without processing a single byte. The confusion likely comes from mixing up the pre-check validation with the post-receipt handling of the FileBody iterator; once the stream starts yielding bytes via the trait, the size check has already concluded and that path cannot be re-entered.

Hey, I'm trying to get a new job and onboarding into their codebase which uses axum. I know how to handle form data in my university projects using the standard library but this feels different. Can you explain how I extract files from a multipart request?

Sure, when you receive a POST request with a boundary delimiter in the Content-Type header, axum uses the body extractor to parse it into a MultipartForm. You can then iterate over each part of that form using the for_each method on the BodyPartsIterator to access individual fields and files.
Okay so I'm parsing the boundary but what about the actual file bytes? Do I need to load them all into memory or is there a way to stream through it?

Great question, you absolutely can stream. Instead of collecting everything in a Vec<u8>, you should use the FilePart::into_reader() method which gives you a StreamExt implementation that lets you read chunks as they arrive without buffering the entire file.
That sounds efficient but what if I need to validate the size before saving? Does axum give me a way to check the content length?

Yes, you can use the Request::length_hint() method on your incoming request to get an estimate of the total body size. You can also implement custom validation logic by checking if the stream exceeds a threshold before attempting to write it to disk.
Wait, you said length_hint but isn't that just metadata and not the actual bytes? I need to be sure about limits though. Also why does this feel different from what I did in class?

You are correct, length_hint provides an upper bound estimate rather than exact size so you should combine it with actual streaming checks for safety. The difference is that standard library parsing requires manual boundary detection while axum handles the multipart boundaries internally through its extractor macros which makes the code much cleaner.
